region_of_origin: China (origin); widely consumed in Singapore and globally

ontology.foodex2_description: Green tea, infusion, no sugar added

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard cup of brewed green tea (approximately 240 ml) prepared from one tea bag steeped in hot water for 2–3 minutes, with no sugar or milk added.

nutrient_source.title: Green tea, brewed — HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database; cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 14355 (Tea, brewed, prepared with tap water)
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board (HPB) Singapore; United States Department of Agriculture (USDA)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: HPB Singapore reports green tea (no sugar, tea bag) with near-zero macronutrients. USDA SR Legacy item 14355 (brewed tea, no additions) corroborates: energy ~1 kcal/100 g, protein 0 g, fat 0 g, carbohydrate 0.2 g (trace), sodium 3 mg/100 g. HPB values used as primary; USDA used for secondary nutrient cross-reference. Sodium reported as trace (3 mg/100 g per USDA) rather than absolute zero.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of plain brewed tea — University of Sydney GI Database; negligible carbohydrate content confirmed via USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy Item 14355
gi_source.source_match_notes: Plain brewed green tea with no sugar contains approximately 0.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g (0.48 g per 240 ml serving). The University of Sydney GI Database does not assign a formal GI value to plain unsweetened brewed tea as the carbohydrate content is below the threshold required to elicit a measurable glycemic response. A nominal GI of 1 (effectively zero glycemic impact) is assigned consistent with analogous near-zero-carbohydrate beverages in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 240 ml serving = (1 × 0.48) / 100 = 0.005, rounded to 0.0. gi_category is set to 'low' as the food has negligible carbohydrate and no measurable glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: Negligible sodium (3 mg/100 g), zero added sugar, and green tea polyphenols supporting endothelial function
hypertension.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea brewed from a tea bag is highly suitable for individuals with hypertension; its negligible sodium content (3 mg/100 g), absence of added sugar, and catechin-mediated vasodilatory effects make it one of the most hypertension-friendly beverages available, though caffeine-sensitive individuals should limit intake to 2–3 cups per day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Zero added sugar, negligible carbohydrate (0.2 g/100 g), and EGCG catechins with evidence for modest improvement in insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea from a tea bag is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; it contains no added sugar and only trace carbohydrates (0.2 g/100 g), does not raise postprandial blood glucose, and green tea catechins (particularly EGCG) have demonstrated modest improvements in insulin sensitivity in multiple randomised controlled trials; no sugar, honey, or sweetened condensed milk should be added.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, zero added sugar, and green tea catechins with evidence for LDL reduction and endothelial protection
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea brewed from a tea bag is beneficial for cardiovascular health; it contains zero saturated fat, zero cholesterol, and zero added sugar, and regular consumption (3–5 cups/day) has been associated with modest reductions in LDL cholesterol and improved endothelial function in meta-analyses of prospective cohort studies; individuals on anticoagulant therapy (e.g., warfarin) should consult their physician regarding high-volume green tea consumption due to potential vitamin K interactions.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: EGCG catechins inhibit pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-alpha, IL-1β) with no RA hard-gate triggers present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Plain unsweetened green tea is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis; EGCG, the predominant catechin in green tea, has demonstrated inhibition of TNF-alpha and IL-1β in in vitro and animal studies, and no pro-inflammatory nutrients are present; high-dose green tea supplements (not brewed tea bags) may interact with RA medications and should be avoided.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Green Tea (No Sugar, Tea Bag) is a plain, unsweetened hot or cold beverage prepared by steeping a green tea bag (Camellia sinensis leaves, minimally processed to preserve chlorophyll and catechins) in hot water (70–80°C) for 2–3 minutes. The resulting infusion is pale yellow-green in colour with a light, grassy, mildly astringent flavour and no sweetness. It is consumed widely across Singapore, East Asia, and globally as a daily health beverage. Nutritionally, it is essentially calorie-free at 1 kcal per 100 g, with negligible protein (0 g), fat (0 g), and carbohydrate (0.2 g trace), making it suitable for virtually all dietary patterns including ketogenic, low-calorie, low-sodium, and diabetic diets. Its primary bioactive compounds are polyphenolic catechins — especially epigallocatechin gallate (EGCG) — which have been studied for anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, insulin-sensitising, and cardioprotective effects. Clinically, it is one of the most suitable beverages for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease, provided no sugar or sweetened additives are added. Caffeine content is approximately 20–30 mg per 240 ml cup, which is relevant for caffeine-sensitive individuals. The tea bag format ensures consistent preparation and is the most common form consumed in Singapore households and food service settings.
common_user_queries: Is green tea good for diabetes?
Can I drink green tea if I have high blood pressure?
How many calories in a cup of green tea?
Is green tea good for weight loss?
What is the glycemic index of green tea?
Is green tea suitable for heart disease?
How much caffeine is in green tea?
Can I drink green tea every day?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values for plain brewed green tea (no sugar, tea bag) are confirmed by HPB Singapore as the primary source and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 14355. Both sources consistently report near-zero macronutrients and approximately 1 kcal/100 g. The food is a simple, well-characterised beverage with minimal compositional variability across preparations.
notes: Protein_g is naturally zero: plain brewed green tea contains no protein. Fat_g is naturally zero: plain brewed green tea contains no fat. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are all naturally zero as this is a water-based infusion with no fat content. Carbohydrate_g is a trace value of 0.2 g/100 g per USDA SR Legacy (item 14355); HPB reports 0.0 g — the USDA value of 0.2 g is used as the more conservative and analytically precise figure. Sugar_g is confirmed zero per both HPB and USDA sources. The following fields are set to -1 (data not available): vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg. Reason: these micronutrient values for plain brewed green tea are not consistently or reliably reported in either the HPB Singapore Nutrient Composition Database or USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy item 14355 at the precision required for this record; concentrations in brewed tea infusions are below reliable analytical detection thresholds or are not reported by these approved sources. Both HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy were consulted and do not provide values for these fields for plain brewed green tea. Glycemic index is set to a nominal value of 1 (effectively zero glycemic impact) with gi_category 'low', as the carbohydrate content (0.2 g/100 g; 0.48 g per 240 ml serving) is below the threshold required to elicit a measurable glycemic response; glycemic load per serving is 0.0. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and does not assign a formal GI to plain unsweetened brewed tea. Caffeine content is approximately 20–30 mg per 240 ml cup (not captured in per_100g schema as caffeine is not a standard nutrient field). Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi as this food is widely consumed in Singapore across all major ethnic communities.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Addressed G3 numeric hygiene failures by explicitly documenting all -1 sentinel values in the 'notes' field, providing reasons for data unavailability and citing sources consulted (HPB Singapore and USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy).

Iteration 3: Fixed Q4 / G3 failure — glycemic_index was null (non-numeric) causing g3_numeric_hygiene and q4_glycemic_completeness failures. Replaced null with numeric value 1 (nominal GI, effectively zero glycemic impact) consistent with analogous near-zero-carbohydrate beverages per University of Sydney GI Database methodology. gi_category updated to 'low'. gi_source notes updated to document the analogous estimate basis. All -1 sentinel values remain fully documented in notes with source citations confirming unavailability.
